enrichment.short_description: AstroNvim is a free, open-source Neovim configuration framework that turns the plain text editor into a full-featured, IDE-like coding environment out of the box.
enrichment.seo_title: AstroNvim Review 2026: Features, Setup and Plugins
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AstroNvim is a free, open-source Neovim config framework with built-in LSP, autocompletion and file explorer. See features, setup steps and community plugins for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AstroNvim is a free and open-source configuration framework for Neovim, the terminal-based text editor descended from Vim. Rather than being a standalone application, AstroNvim is a curated, pre-built collection of Neovim plugins and settings, distributed as a Lua-based configuration repository on GitHub, that transforms a bare Neovim install into a polished, IDE-like development environment with almost no manual setup. It is maintained by the AstroNvim open-source organization and a volunteer community rather than a commercial company, and it has no pricing, subscriptions, or paid tiers since it is entirely free software released publicly on GitHub. The project has become one of the most popular Neovim distributions, alongside alternatives like LazyVim and LunarVim, and has attracted tens of thousands of GitHub stars along with an active Discord server and community-run plugin marketplace called AstroCommunity, which offers more than 40 ready-to-install language packs. Core features include automatic language server, linter, and formatter installation through the bundled Mason package manager, a Neo-tree file explorer, Treesitter-based syntax highlighting, Gitsigns git integration, a Heirline-powered statusline and bufferline, integrated terminal via Toggleterm, fuzzy finding, and native Language Server Protocol and Debug Adapter Protocol support. AstroNvim's architecture is modular: its configuration is built as a set of individual plugin specs that behave like ordinary Neovim plugins, so users can add, remove, or override any piece without learning a bespoke configuration language. Extensive lazy-loading keeps startup times fast even with a large plugin set. The target audience is developers, students, and terminal power users who want a fast, keyboard-driven coding environment without hand-assembling a Neovim config from scratch, but who still want full control to customize it. Compared to VS Code or JetBrains IDEs, AstroNvim is far lighter-weight, runs entirely in the terminal, and requires more comfort with keyboard-centric workflows, but it starts near-instantly and consumes a fraction of the memory. Compared to other Neovim distributions like LazyVim, AstroNvim is generally considered slightly more configuration-heavy and community-plugin-driven, while LunarVim and NvChad take somewhat different structural approaches. Because it is entirely open source under a permissive license and requires no account, signup, or payment, AstroNvim carries no vendor lock-in risk: users can always fork the configuration or eject to a plain Neovim setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AstroNvim
paragraphs: AstroNvim is an open-source configuration framework for Neovim, the modernized fork of the classic Vim text editor. Instead of shipping as its own application, AstroNvim is a set of Lua configuration files and curated plugins that developers install on top of Neovim to get a ready-made, IDE-like editing experience.

The project positions itself as a middle ground between a completely bare Neovim install, which requires hours of manual configuration, and a heavyweight IDE. It ships with sensible defaults for language support, file navigation, git integration, and UI polish, while still exposing every setting so advanced users can customize it freely.

AstroNvim is developed in the open on GitHub and maintained by a community of contributors rather than a company, with support channels on Discord and social platforms where users can ask questions and share configurations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, AstroNvim bundles automatic tooling setup through the Mason package manager, so installing a language server, linter, or formatter for a new programming language takes a single command rather than manual configuration.

Navigation and editing are handled by well-known Neovim plugins wired together out of the box: Neo-tree for the file explorer, Treesitter for syntax-aware highlighting, a fuzzy finder for searching files and text, and Toggleterm for an integrated terminal panel.

AstroNvim also includes git integration through Gitsigns, a customizable statusline and bufferline built with Heirline, native Language Server Protocol support for code intelligence, and Debug Adapter Protocol support for step-through debugging.

The AstroCommunity plugin marketplace extends this further, offering dozens of drop-in language packs and plugin bundles that users can enable with a single import line rather than writing configuration from scratch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AstroNvim is completely free and open source. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, license keys, or account requirements of any kind.

Because it runs on top of Neovim, the only cost is the time required to install Neovim itself and, optionally, language-specific tooling such as compilers or language servers, most of which are also free and open source.

features.title: Automatic tooling setup
features.description: The bundled Mason package manager installs language servers, linters, and formatters with a single command, removing manual configuration for new languages.

features.title: AstroCommunity plugin marketplace
features.description: A community-maintained repository of more than 40 drop-in language packs and plugin bundles that can be enabled with one import line.

features.title: File explorer and fuzzy finding
features.description: Neo-tree provides a tree-style file explorer, while a built-in fuzzy finder speeds up searching files, text, and commands.

features.title: Git integration
features.description: Gitsigns shows inline diff markers and git status directly in the editor gutter.

features.title: LSP and DAP support
features.description: Native Language Server Protocol support enables code completion, diagnostics, and go-to-definition, while Debug Adapter Protocol support enables step-through debugging.

features.title: Fast startup via lazy loading
features.description: Plugins are loaded only when needed, keeping startup times low even with a large plugin set installed.

features.title: Modular, plugin-style configuration
features.description: Every part of the config is structured as a standard Neovim plugin spec, so users can override or remove pieces without learning a custom configuration DSL.

features.title: Integrated terminal and UI polish
features.description: Toggleterm provides an in-editor terminal, while a Heirline-powered statusline, winbar, and bufferline give a clean, informative interface.

pros: Completely free and open source with no account or payment required
Very fast startup and low resource usage compared to full IDEs
Large community plugin ecosystem (AstroCommunity) covers most popular languages
Highly customizable without needing to build a config from zero
Active Discord community and frequent updates
cons: Steeper learning curve than a graphical IDE for developers unfamiliar with Vim keybindings
Requires comfort with the terminal and text-based configuration files
Occasional breaking changes between major versions can require migration steps
Troubleshooting plugin conflicts can be harder than with a single-vendor IDE
No official commercial support channel since it is a volunteer-run project

faqs.question: Is AstroNvim free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. AstroNvim is fully open source and free, with no paid tiers or account requirements.

faqs.question: Do I need to know Vim to use AstroNvim
faqs.answer: Basic familiarity with Vim-style modal editing helps, but AstroNvim's documentation and defaults are designed to make onboarding easier than configuring Neovim from scratch.

faqs.question: What is the difference between AstroNvim and plain Neovim
faqs.answer: Neovim is the base text editor; AstroNvim is a pre-built configuration framework installed on top of it that adds file navigation, language support, git integration, and UI out of the box.

faqs.question: How does AstroNvim compare to LazyVim or LunarVim
faqs.answer: All three are popular Neovim distributions with similar goals. They differ mainly in default plugin choices, structure, and community conventions, so the best fit often comes down to personal preference.

faqs.question: Does AstroNvim support every programming language
faqs.answer: Language support is added through Mason and the AstroCommunity plugin packs, which cover most mainstream languages; less common languages may require manual language server configuration.

faqs.question: Can I customize AstroNvim heavily
faqs.answer: Yes. Its modular, plugin-style architecture is designed so any default can be overridden, replaced, or removed in a user configuration file.

faqs.question: What operating systems does AstroNvim run on
faqs.answer: AstroNvim runs anywhere Neovim runs, including Linux, macOS, and Windows (including via WSL).

faqs.question: Is there official support if something breaks
faqs.answer: Support comes from the community via the project's Discord server and GitHub issue tracker rather than a paid support contract.

use_cases.title: Full-time software development
use_cases.description: Developers use AstroNvim as a daily-driver coding environment with LSP-powered autocompletion, diagnostics, and debugging across multiple languages.

use_cases.title: Lightweight remote and terminal-only editing
use_cases.description: Because it runs entirely in the terminal, AstroNvim is well suited to editing code over SSH on remote servers or low-resource machines.

use_cases.title: Learning Vim-style editing with modern conveniences
use_cases.description: Students and hobbyists use AstroNvim to learn modal editing while still getting IDE-like conveniences like autocomplete and file trees.

enrichment.short_description: ASUS is a Taiwanese multinational technology company that designs and manufactures laptops, desktops, motherboards, graphics cards, monitors, networking gear, and the ROG gaming brand.
enrichment.seo_title: ASUS Overview 2026: Products, History and Hardware Lineup
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ASUS is a Taiwan-based hardware maker known for motherboards, laptops, ROG gaming gear, monitors and routers. See its history, product lines and scale for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ASUS, formally ASUSTeK Computer Inc, is a Taiwanese multinational computer hardware and electronics company founded in Taipei in 1989 by four engineers, Tzu-Hsien Tung, Ted Hsu, Wayne Hsieh, and M.T. Liao, who had previously worked together at Acer. The company started by building PC motherboards and grew into one of the world's largest technology hardware manufacturers, headquartered in the Beitou District of Taipei, Taiwan, with tens of thousands of employees globally and tens of billions of dollars in annual revenue. ASUS is not a single software product but a diversified hardware company whose relevant product lines for a technology directory include desktop and laptop motherboards, prebuilt and custom desktop PCs, Windows laptops (including the ZenBook and VivoBook lines), the Eee PC netbook series that helped popularize affordable ultraportable laptops starting in 2007, monitors and displays, graphics cards, networking equipment such as WiFi routers and mesh systems, and components for creators and professionals under lines like ProArt. Its best-known sub-brand is Republic of Gamers, or ROG, launched in 2006 and now one of the most recognized names in PC gaming hardware, spanning gaming laptops, motherboards, graphics cards, monitors, peripherals, and handheld gaming devices, alongside sponsorship of esports teams and tournaments. ASUS also owns a majority stake in graphics card and motherboard maker ASRock and has historically had close ties to chip and device joint ventures in the Taiwanese electronics supply chain. Because ASUS sells thousands of individual SKUs across wildly different price points, from budget routers and entry motherboards to flagship gaming laptops and workstation-class hardware, there is no single starting price or subscription model; pricing is set per product and varies by region and retailer. The company competes broadly with other PC and component makers such as Acer, Lenovo, Dell, HP, MSI, and Gigabyte, and within gaming specifically ROG competes with brand lines like MSI's own gaming division and Razer. ASUS's core audience spans everyday consumers buying laptops and routers, PC enthusiasts and gamers building or buying systems, and IT departments and system integrators sourcing motherboards and components at scale. The company is publicly traded on the Taiwan Stock Exchange and remains one of the most influential hardware brands in the global PC ecosystem, particularly in motherboards and gaming laptops.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is ASUS
paragraphs: ASUS (ASUSTeK Computer Inc) is a Taiwanese multinational hardware manufacturer founded in Taipei in 1989 by four former Acer engineers. What began as a motherboard maker has grown into one of the largest PC hardware companies in the world, with a product portfolio spanning laptops, desktops, components, displays, networking gear, and gaming hardware.

The company is headquartered in the Beitou District of Taipei, Taiwan, and is publicly traded on the Taiwan Stock Exchange. It employs tens of thousands of people globally and generates tens of billions of dollars in annual revenue.

In a software and technology directory context, ASUS is most relevant as the hardware layer underneath much of the PC and gaming ecosystem, manufacturing the machines, motherboards, and peripherals that run consumer and professional software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Product Lines
paragraphs: ASUS's core business remains motherboards, spanning entry-level boards to high-end enthusiast and workstation chipsets, which power both prebuilt systems and custom PC builds worldwide.

Its laptop lineup includes the ZenBook series for premium thin-and-light laptops, the VivoBook series for mainstream consumers, and the ProArt line aimed at creative professionals, alongside the Eee PC, which helped popularize affordable netbooks starting in 2007.

Republic of Gamers, or ROG, launched in 2006, is ASUS's dedicated gaming sub-brand covering gaming laptops, desktops, motherboards, graphics cards, monitors, peripherals, and handheld gaming devices, and has become one of the most recognized names in PC gaming hardware.

Beyond computers, ASUS also produces monitors and displays, WiFi routers and mesh networking systems, graphics cards, and other components, making it a broad hardware ecosystem provider rather than a single-product company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ASUS does not have a single price point or subscription model. Pricing varies enormously by product category and configuration, from budget routers and entry-level motherboards costing well under a hundred dollars to flagship ROG gaming laptops and workstation hardware that can run several thousand dollars.
Products are sold through retail partners, ASUS's own online store, and system integrators, with regional pricing that varies by market.

features.title: Motherboards
features.description: ASUS is one of the world's largest motherboard makers, spanning entry-level to high-end enthusiast and workstation chipsets.

features.title: ZenBook and VivoBook laptops
features.description: Consumer and premium Windows laptop lines covering ultraportables, mainstream laptops, and creator-focused ProArt machines.

features.title: Republic of Gamers (ROG)
features.description: A dedicated gaming sub-brand covering laptops, desktops, motherboards, graphics cards, monitors, and peripherals.

features.title: Monitors and displays
features.description: A wide range of consumer, professional, and gaming displays, including high-refresh-rate ROG panels.

features.title: Networking hardware
features.description: WiFi routers and mesh networking systems sold under both the ASUS and ROG brands.

features.title: Graphics cards
features.description: Discrete GPUs built around NVIDIA and AMD chipsets, sold under both mainstream and ROG Strix branding.

features.title: Handheld gaming devices
features.description: The ROG Ally line of handheld gaming PCs designed for portable PC gaming.

features.title: Global manufacturing scale
features.description: Decades of manufacturing experience and supply chain relationships across the Taiwanese electronics industry.

pros: Extremely broad hardware ecosystem spanning motherboards, laptops, components, and networking gear
Strong reputation in PC gaming through the ROG brand
Decades of manufacturing experience and engineering credibility, especially in motherboards
Wide range of price points, from budget to premium enthusiast hardware
Global retail and support presence
cons: Product quality and support experience can vary by category and region
No unified pricing or subscription model, since it sells discrete hardware products rather than a software service
Some product lines face strong competition from equally established rivals like Lenovo, Dell, and MSI
Warranty and after-sales support quality has drawn mixed reviews in some regions

faqs.question: Where is ASUS headquartered
faqs.answer: ASUS is headquartered in the Beitou District of Taipei, Taiwan.

faqs.question: When was ASUS founded
faqs.answer: ASUS was founded in 1989 in Taipei by four engineers who had previously worked at Acer.

faqs.question: What is ROG
faqs.answer: Republic of Gamers (ROG) is ASUS's gaming hardware sub-brand, launched in 2006, covering gaming laptops, desktops, components, and peripherals.

faqs.question: Does ASUS only make laptops
faqs.answer: No. ASUS makes a broad range of hardware including motherboards, desktops, laptops, monitors, graphics cards, routers, and gaming peripherals.

faqs.question: How much do ASUS products cost
faqs.answer: Pricing varies widely by product category, from budget routers and motherboards to flagship ROG gaming laptops costing several thousand dollars.

faqs.question: Is ASUS a publicly traded company
faqs.answer: Yes, ASUS is publicly traded on the Taiwan Stock Exchange.

faqs.question: What is ASUS's relationship to ASRock
faqs.answer: ASUS holds a stake in ASRock, another motherboard and hardware manufacturer that originated as an ASUS spin-off.

use_cases.title: Building or buying a gaming PC
use_cases.description: Gamers use ROG-branded motherboards, graphics cards, laptops, and monitors to build high-performance gaming systems.

use_cases.title: Everyday computing and productivity
use_cases.description: Consumers and professionals buy ZenBook and VivoBook laptops for general productivity, study, and creative work.

use_cases.title: System integration and IT procurement
use_cases.description: System builders and IT departments source ASUS motherboards, components, and networking hardware at scale.

enrichment.short_description: Athenahealth is a healthcare technology company providing cloud-based electronic health records, practice management, and medical billing software for medical practices and health systems.
enrichment.seo_title: Athenahealth Review 2026: EHR, Billing and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Athenahealth offers cloud-based EHR, practice management, and medical billing software for healthcare providers. See athenaOne features, history, and pricing for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Athenahealth is a healthcare technology company that provides cloud-based electronic health records (EHR), practice management, medical billing, and patient engagement software to medical practices and health systems across the United States. The company was founded in 1997 by Jonathan Bush and Todd Park, originally starting as a women's health and birthing practice in San Diego before pivoting into healthcare software; it later relocated its headquarters to the Boston, Massachusetts area and went public in 2007. Athenahealth's flagship offering today is athenaOne, an integrated cloud-based suite that bundles athenaClinicals, its electronic health records and clinical documentation module, athenaCollector, its revenue cycle management and medical billing service, and athenaCommunicator, its patient engagement and care coordination tools, alongside the Epocrates mobile clinical reference app it acquired in 2013. The company has grown substantially through acquisitions, including RazorInsights and webOMR technology in 2015, and underwent major ownership changes in the years since: activist investor Elliott Management pushed for a sale in 2018, and Veritas Capital and Evergreen Coast Capital acquired athenahealth for approximately 5.7 billion dollars in a deal completed in February 2019, merging it with Virence Health, the former GE Healthcare IT business. In February 2022, Bain Capital and Hellman and Friedman acquired athenahealth for roughly 17 billion dollars, one of the largest private equity transactions in healthcare IT history, and the company has remained privately held since. As of recent company disclosures, athenahealth employs around 8,800 people and its network connects more than 170,000 providers, processing over 315 million medical claims annually and touching more than a fifth of the US population through its client base. Athenahealth targets ambulatory healthcare practices of varying sizes, from small independent practices to larger multi-specialty groups and health systems, and it does not publish list pricing; prospective customers get custom quotes based on practice size, specialty, and which modules (EHR, billing, patient engagement) they adopt, often combined with a percentage-of-collections billing model for its revenue cycle services rather than a flat per-user subscription fee. Its main competitors in the ambulatory EHR and practice management space include Epic, Cerner (Oracle Health), eClinicalWorks, NextGen Healthcare, and Kareo (Tebra), with athenahealth generally positioned as a cloud-native, services-heavy alternative that bundles billing operations support alongside its software rather than selling software alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Athenahealth
paragraphs: Athenahealth is a healthcare technology company that builds cloud-based software for medical practices, covering electronic health records, practice management, medical billing, and patient engagement. It was founded in 1997 by Jonathan Bush and Todd Park and is now headquartered in the Boston, Massachusetts area.

The company went public in 2007 and later went through two major private-equity ownership changes: Veritas Capital and Evergreen Coast Capital acquired it in 2019 in a deal that merged it with GE Healthcare's former IT business, Virence Health, and Bain Capital together with Hellman and Friedman acquired it again in 2022 for roughly 17 billion dollars.

Athenahealth serves ambulatory healthcare practices, from small independent physician offices to larger multi-specialty groups, connecting more than 170,000 providers and processing hundreds of millions of medical claims each year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features and Product Suite
paragraphs: The flagship product, athenaOne, is an integrated cloud-based platform that bundles several previously separate modules into a single suite for medical practices.
athenaClinicals is the electronic health records and clinical documentation component, supporting charting, e-prescribing, and clinical decision support at the point of care.

athenaCollector handles revenue cycle management and medical billing, including claims scrubbing and submission, denial management, and collections tracking, often paired with a percentage-of-collections pricing model.

athenaCommunicator provides patient engagement tools such as appointment reminders, online scheduling, and secure messaging, while the Epocrates app, acquired in 2013, offers point-of-care clinical reference for prescribers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Athenahealth does not publish standard list pricing. Costs depend on practice size, specialty, which modules are adopted, and deployment scope, and are quoted directly through the sales team.

For its revenue cycle management services, athenahealth has historically used a percentage-of-collections model in addition to or instead of flat subscription fees, meaning cost scales with a practice's billed revenue rather than a fixed per-user rate.

features.title: athenaClinicals (EHR)
features.description: Cloud-based electronic health records covering clinical documentation, charting, and e-prescribing.

features.title: athenaCollector (billing)
features.description: Revenue cycle management and medical billing service including claims submission and denial management.

features.title: athenaCommunicator (patient engagement)
features.description: Appointment reminders, online scheduling, and secure patient messaging tools.

features.title: athenaOne integrated suite
features.description: A single cloud platform combining EHR, billing, and patient engagement modules for ambulatory practices.

features.title: Epocrates clinical reference
features.description: A mobile point-of-care drug and clinical reference app used by prescribers, acquired by athenahealth in 2013.

features.title: Large provider network
features.description: A network of more than 170,000 connected providers that supports benchmarking and shared payer rule intelligence.

features.title: Claims processing at scale
features.description: The platform processes hundreds of millions of medical claims annually across its client base.

pros: Cloud-based delivery removes the need for practices to self-host or maintain on-premises servers
Integrated EHR, billing, and patient engagement reduces the need for separate point solutions
Large provider network can surface payer rule updates and claims intelligence across clients
Established, well-funded company with decades of healthcare IT experience
Revenue cycle services can offload billing operations work from smaller practices
cons: Pricing is not public, requiring a sales conversation to get a quote
Percentage-of-collections billing can be more expensive than flat fees for high-revenue practices
As with most full-featured EHR platforms, the learning curve can be steep for new staff
Practice-reported experiences with implementation and support vary by specialty and practice size

faqs.question: What does athenahealth do
faqs.answer: Athenahealth provides cloud-based electronic health records, practice management, medical billing, and patient engagement software for medical practices and health systems.

faqs.question: Is athenahealth publicly traded
faqs.answer: No. Athenahealth was public from 2007 until 2019, when it was taken private by Veritas Capital and Evergreen Coast Capital; it was acquired again by Bain Capital and Hellman and Friedman in 2022 and remains privately held.

faqs.question: What is athenaOne
faqs.answer: athenaOne is athenahealth's integrated cloud platform that bundles athenaClinicals, athenaCollector, and athenaCommunicator into a single suite.

faqs.question: How much does athenahealth cost
faqs.answer: Athenahealth does not publish list pricing. Costs are quoted based on practice size, specialty, and modules used, and revenue cycle services are often billed as a percentage of collections.

faqs.question: Who founded athenahealth
faqs.answer: Athenahealth was founded in 1997 by Jonathan Bush and Todd Park.

faqs.question: What is athenaClinicals
faqs.answer: athenaClinicals is athenahealth's electronic health records module, covering clinical documentation, charting, and e-prescribing.

faqs.question: Who are athenahealth's main competitors
faqs.answer: Competitors in ambulatory EHR and practice management include Epic, Oracle Health (Cerner), eClinicalWorks, NextGen Healthcare, and Kareo (Tebra).

faqs.question: Where is athenahealth headquartered
faqs.answer: Athenahealth is headquartered in the Boston, Massachusetts area.

use_cases.title: Ambulatory practice EHR and charting

use_cases.audience: Physicians and clinical staff at medical practices

use_cases.description: Practice administrators use athenaCollector to submit claims, manage denials, and track collections.

features: Pricing based on practice size, specialty, and modules selected
Revenue cycle services often billed as a percentage of collections

enrichment.short_description: Attendize is a free, open-source, self-hosted ticket selling and event management platform built on Laravel, offering an alternative to paid platforms like Eventbrite.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Attendize is a free, self-hosted, open-source event ticketing platform built on Laravel. See its features, setup requirements, and current maintenance status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Attendize is an open-source event ticketing and management platform built on the Laravel PHP framework, distributed as free code on GitHub rather than as a hosted commercial product. It positions itself as a self-hosted alternative to paid ticketing platforms such as Eventbrite, letting event organizers sell tickets and manage attendees on their own servers without paying per-ticket service fees to a third party. Core features include mobile-friendly, customizable event pages, attendee management with refund and messaging tools, customizable tickets with QR codes and organizer branding, data export of attendee lists to formats like CSV and XLS, real-time event statistics, support for unlimited organizers and events on a single install, multiple currency support, affiliate tracking to measure referral sales, widget support for embedding ticket-selling widgets into external websites or WordPress blogs, and integration with payment gateways such as Stripe and PayPal to process transactions directly. The platform is released under the Attribution Assurance License and requires a standard LAMP-style stack to self-host, including PHP 7.1.3 or higher, MySQL 5.7 or higher, and several common PHP extensions, with Docker-based deployment options available to simplify setup. Because it is purely self-hosted open-source software, Attendize has no company behind it in the traditional sense, no disclosed founding year, headquarters, or employee count, and no official hosted SaaS version at attendize.com; some third-party providers offer managed hosting for Attendize as a paid convenience service, and a white-label commercial licensing option has historically been referenced in its documentation. Development activity has slowed significantly: the most recent tagged release, version 2.8.0, dates to early 2023, and the GitHub repository has a large backlog of open issues and pull requests, which means the project should be considered lightly maintained rather than actively developed, and self-hosters should expect to handle security patching themselves. Attendize is best suited to technically capable organizers, developers, or small event companies who want full control and no per-ticket fees and are comfortable managing their own server, database, and updates, as opposed to those who want a fully managed, actively supported SaaS ticketing product. Its natural comparison points are other self-hosted or open-source event platforms, and on the commercial SaaS side, Eventbrite, Tito, and Hi.Events.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Attendize
paragraphs: Attendize is a free, open-source ticket selling and event management platform built on the Laravel PHP framework. Rather than being offered as a hosted SaaS product, it is distributed as source code that organizers install and run on their own server, giving them full control over their event data and avoiding per-ticket fees charged by third-party ticketing platforms.
The project is released under the Attribution Assurance License and is available on GitHub, where anyone can download, deploy, and modify it.

Development activity has slowed considerably in recent years; the most recent tagged release dates back to early 2023, so the platform is best understood today as a mature but lightly maintained open-source project rather than an actively developed commercial product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Attendize covers the core workflow of event ticketing: creating customizable, mobile-friendly event pages, issuing tickets with QR codes and organizer branding, and managing attendees including refunds and bulk messaging.
Organizers can run unlimited events and organizers from a single install, accept multiple currencies, and track affiliate or referral sales to see which channels are driving ticket purchases.

The platform integrates with payment gateways including Stripe and PayPal, exports attendee lists to formats like CSV and XLS, and provides real-time statistics dashboards along with embeddable widgets for selling tickets directly from an external website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Attendize itself is free and open source with no license fee. The only costs involved are the hosting infrastructure (a server running PHP and MySQL) and any payment gateway processing fees charged by providers like Stripe or PayPal.

Some third-party providers offer paid managed hosting for Attendize for organizers who do not want to self-host, and the project's documentation has historically referenced an optional white-label commercial licensing arrangement.

features.title: Customizable event pages
features.description: Mobile-friendly, brandable event pages for each event created on the platform.

features.title: Attendee management
features.description: Tools for managing attendee lists, issuing refunds, and sending bulk messages to ticket holders.

features.title: QR code ticketing
features.description: Tickets include QR codes and organizer branding for check-in scanning at events.

features.title: Payment gateway integration
features.description: Built-in support for processing payments through Stripe and PayPal.

features.title: Multi-currency and affiliate tracking
features.description: Support for selling in multiple currencies and tracking affiliate or referral-driven ticket sales.

features.title: Data export and reporting
features.description: Export of attendee data to CSV and XLS, plus real-time event statistics dashboards.

features.title: Embeddable ticket widgets
features.description: Widgets that let organizers sell tickets directly from an external website or WordPress blog.

features.title: Unlimited organizers and events
features.description: A single self-hosted install can manage unlimited organizer accounts and events.

pros: Free and open source with no per-ticket platform fees
Full data ownership since it is self-hosted
Feature set covers the core needs of ticketing: pages, payments, check-in, and reporting
No vendor lock-in; source code can be modified freely
Docker-based deployment options simplify initial setup
cons: Development has slowed significantly, with the latest release dating to early 2023
Requires technical skill to self-host, secure, and maintain (PHP, MySQL, server administration)
No official actively-supported hosted version from the original project
Security patches are not guaranteed given the reduced maintenance activity
Large backlog of open issues on GitHub suggests some rough edges remain unresolved

faqs.question: Is Attendize free
faqs.answer: Yes. Attendize is open-source software and free to download and self-host; the only costs are your own hosting and any payment processor fees.

faqs.question: Does Attendize offer a hosted SaaS version
faqs.answer: Not officially from the core project. Some third-party providers offer paid managed hosting for Attendize, but there is no first-party hosted SaaS equivalent to attendize.com.

faqs.question: Is Attendize still actively maintained
faqs.answer: Development has slowed considerably; the most recent tagged release was version 2.8.0 in early 2023, so it should be considered lightly maintained.

faqs.question: What do I need to self-host Attendize
faqs.answer: A server running PHP 7.1.3 or higher, MySQL 5.7 or higher, and several standard PHP extensions; Docker-based deployment is also supported.

faqs.question: What payment gateways does Attendize support
faqs.answer: Attendize integrates with Stripe and PayPal for processing ticket payments.

faqs.question: What license is Attendize released under
faqs.answer: Attendize is released under the Attribution Assurance License.

faqs.question: How does Attendize compare to Eventbrite
faqs.answer: Eventbrite is a hosted SaaS platform that charges per-ticket fees, while Attendize is self-hosted and free, trading convenience and managed support for full control and no platform fees.

faqs.question: Can I embed ticket sales on my own website with Attendize
faqs.answer: Yes, Attendize provides embeddable widgets for selling tickets directly from an external website or WordPress blog.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted ticketing without platform fees
use_cases.description: Organizers who want to avoid per-ticket fees charged by commercial platforms deploy Attendize on their own server.
use_cases.audience: Independent event organizers and small event companies

use_cases.title: Custom-branded event registration
use_cases.description: Organizations that need full control over event page branding and attendee data use Attendize's customizable pages and data export tools.

use_cases.title: Developer-extended ticketing systems
use_cases.description: Developers fork or extend the open-source codebase to build a ticketing system tailored to specific business requirements.

enrichment.short_description: Audio Enhancer is a free, browser-based AI audio cleanup tool from Voice Clone that removes background noise, echo, and clicks, and levels loudness in spoken-word recordings.
enrichment.seo_title: Audio Enhancer Review 2026: Free AI Voice Cleanup Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Audio Enhancer is a free AI tool from Voice Clone that removes noise, echo, and clicks from voice recordings. See features, supported formats, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Audio Enhancer is a free, browser-based audio cleanup tool offered at voice-clone.org/tools/audio-enhancer, part of a broader suite of AI-powered voice and audio tools published under the Voice Clone brand. It is designed specifically for spoken-word audio such as podcasts, online courses, interviews, and voiceovers rather than music production, and it uses AI-driven restoration to clean up common recording problems while trying to preserve a natural sound rather than over-processing the audio into a metallic or artificial tone. Its core capabilities include noise reduction that removes background hiss, hum, air-conditioning noise, and traffic sounds while keeping speech intelligible; echo and reverb control that reduces small-room reverberation and flutter echo; light de-essing that softens harsh sibilant sounds like 's' and 'sh'; click and pop repair that removes minor digital glitches and microphone artifacts; and loudness leveling that normalizes uneven speaker volumes to a consistent, comfortable perceived level, which is particularly useful for multi-speaker recordings like interviews and panel discussions. The workflow is simple and requires no account or login: a user uploads an audio file, the AI enhancer processes it, the tool offers a before-and-after preview so the user can compare results, and then the cleaned file can be downloaded. It supports MP3, WAV, FLAC, and OGG file formats, handles files up to roughly 50MB, supports sample rates from 22.05 kHz to 48 kHz, works with both mono and stereo audio, and performs best on clips in the 30 to 120 second range for faster processing. According to the tool's own privacy notes, uploaded files are processed temporarily and automatically cleared after processing, and audio is not retained for model training. As a free tool with no visible premium tier, Audio Enhancer's pricing model is straightforwardly free, and no company size, founding year, or headquarters information is publicly disclosed for Voice Clone. It competes with other browser-based audio cleanup tools such as Adobe Podcast's Enhance Speech, Descript's Studio Sound, and Krisp, and is most useful for creators who need a quick, no-signup way to clean up a single recording rather than a full digital audio workstation or ongoing production pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Audio Enhancer
paragraphs: Audio Enhancer is a free, browser-based AI tool for cleaning up spoken-word audio recordings, published as part of the Voice Clone suite of tools at voice-clone.org. It targets common recording problems in podcasts, interviews, online courses, and voiceovers, such as background noise, room echo, and uneven speaker volume.

The tool is built for quick, single-file cleanup rather than full audio production: users upload a file, the AI processes it, and a cleaned version is available to preview and download within the browser, with no account or software installation required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The tool reduces steady background noise such as hiss, hum, air-conditioning sound, and traffic, while aiming to preserve natural speech quality rather than applying aggressive processing.

It also reduces small-room reverb and flutter echo, softens harsh sibilant sounds through light de-essing, repairs minor clicks and microphone pops, and levels uneven speaker loudness to a consistent, comfortable volume.
A before-and-after preview lets users compare the original and enhanced audio before downloading, and the tool supports MP3, WAV, FLAC, and OGG formats for files up to about 50MB.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Audio Enhancer is free to use directly from the browser, with no login, account creation, or payment required based on the tool's own page.
No separate premium tier or subscription plan is advertised for this specific tool.

features.description: Reduces small-room reverberation and flutter echo common in untreated recording spaces.

features.description: Light smoothing of harsh sibilant sounds like 's' and 'sh' for more comfortable listening.

features.description: Removes minor digital glitches and microphone artifacts from the recording.

features.description: Normalizes uneven speaker volumes to a consistent perceived loudness, useful for interviews and panels.

features.description: Lets users compare the original and enhanced audio before downloading the final file.

features.description: Files can be uploaded and processed without creating an account or logging in.

Not a full digital audio workstation, so it lacks manual mixing controls
No disclosed company details (founding year, size, headquarters) for the publisher

faqs.answer: Yes, the tool is free to use directly in the browser with no account required.

faqs.answer: It supports MP3, WAV, FLAC, and OGG files.

faqs.answer: Yes, files up to approximately 50MB are supported.

faqs.question: Does Audio Enhancer work on music as well as voice
faqs.answer: It is optimized specifically for spoken-word audio like podcasts, interviews, and voiceovers rather than music production.

faqs.question: Does the tool store my uploaded audio
faqs.answer: According to the tool's privacy notes, files are processed temporarily and automatically cleared afterward, and audio is not retained for model training.

faqs.question: Do I need to create an account to use it
faqs.answer: No, no login or account creation is required to use Audio Enhancer.

faqs.question: What company makes Audio Enhancer
faqs.answer: It is published as part of the Voice Clone suite of tools at voice-clone.org; detailed company information such as founding year and headquarters is not publicly disclosed.

enrichment.short_description: Audio Recorder is a free, open-source GTK3 application for Linux that records system audio, microphones, and streaming sound, maintained as a community project on Launchpad.
enrichment.seo_title: Audio Recorder Review 2026: Free Linux Recording App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Audio Recorder is a free, open-source GTK3 audio recording app for Linux hosted on Launchpad. See features, supported formats, and installation options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Audio Recorder is a free, open-source desktop application for Linux that records audio from a computer's sound card, microphones, browsers, webcams, and other sources, effectively capturing anything that plays through the system's speakers. It is developed and maintained as a community project hosted on Launchpad, the collaborative development and hosting platform commonly used by Ubuntu and Debian-based projects, with the project registered on Launchpad in January 2011. The application is built with GTK3 and provides a simple graphical interface aimed at straightforward system and microphone audio capture rather than professional multi-track production. It supports multiple output formats, including OGG, FLAC, MP3, and WAV, and includes an advanced timer feature that can start, stop, or pause recording at a specific clock time, after a set time period, when the file size exceeds a configured limit, or based on voice or sound detection and silence thresholds, which is useful for unattended or scheduled recording tasks such as capturing streamed audio or long-running sessions. Audio Recorder is distributed for free with source code available, and installation is primarily handled through a Launchpad-hosted PPA (Personal Package Archive) for Ubuntu and Linux Mint, with packages historically supporting a wide range of Ubuntu releases (roughly versions 15.x through 22.x) and Linux Mint 18.x through 20.x. As a community open-source project rather than a commercial company, it has no disclosed founding company, headquarters, employee count, or pricing beyond being free software; it is maintained by volunteer contributors through the Launchpad platform, with support handled via Launchpad's mailing list, bug tracker, and Q&A system rather than a traditional customer support channel. Audio Recorder competes with other Linux audio capture utilities such as Audacity (which offers more advanced editing but a steeper interface), SoX, and OBS Studio's audio-only recording capabilities, and it is best suited to Linux users who want a lightweight, purpose-built tool for capturing system or microphone audio without the overhead of a full audio editing suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Audio Recorder
paragraphs: Audio Recorder is a free, open-source Linux application for capturing audio from a computer's sound card, microphones, browsers, webcams, or any other source that plays through the system's speakers. It is developed and hosted as a community project on Launchpad, the collaborative platform widely used by Ubuntu and Debian-based open-source projects.

Built with GTK3, the application focuses on straightforward, single-purpose audio capture rather than multi-track editing or production, making it a lightweight utility for users who just need to record system or microphone sound.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Audio Recorder can capture output from the system sound card, microphones, browser audio, webcam audio, and other in-system sources, essentially recording anything audible through the speakers.
It supports several common output formats, including OGG, FLAC, MP3, and WAV, giving users flexibility depending on their intended use for the recording.

An advanced timer system allows recordings to start, stop, or pause at a specific clock time, after a set duration, once a file size limit is reached, or automatically based on voice or sound detection and configurable silence thresholds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Audio Recorder is completely free and open source, with no paid tiers, licenses, or account requirements.
It is typically installed via a Launchpad-hosted PPA on Ubuntu or Linux Mint, or built from source, at no cost.

features.title: System and microphone audio capture
features.description: Records audio from the sound card, microphones, browsers, webcams, and other in-system sources.

features.description: Supports OGG, FLAC, MP3, and WAV output formats.

features.title: Voice and silence detection
features.description: Can start recording on detected sound and pause or stop during silence, based on user-configurable thresholds.

features.title: Simple GTK3 interface
features.description: A lightweight, straightforward graphical interface built for Linux desktop environments.

features.description: Distributed through a Launchpad-hosted PPA for easy installation on Ubuntu and Linux Mint.

Lightweight and simple compared to full audio editing suites
Supports multiple common audio formats
Flexible timer and silence-detection options for unattended recording
Easy installation via PPA on Ubuntu and Linux Mint
cons: Linux-only, with no native Windows or macOS version
No built-in multi-track editing or advanced production features
Interface and feature set have not changed dramatically in recent years
Support relies on community channels rather than dedicated customer support

faqs.question: Is Audio Recorder free
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open-source software with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Audio Recorder support
faqs.answer: It is built specifically for Linux, with official packages for Ubuntu and Linux Mint distributed via a Launchpad PPA.

faqs.question: What audio formats can I record in
faqs.answer: It supports OGG, FLAC, MP3, and WAV output formats.

faqs.question: Can Audio Recorder record system audio, not just a microphone
faqs.answer: Yes, it can capture anything that plays through the system's sound card, including browser and webcam audio, in addition to microphones.

faqs.question: Can I schedule recordings
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a timer that can start, stop, or pause recording at a specific time, after a duration, on a file size limit, or based on sound and silence detection.

faqs.question: How do I install Audio Recorder
faqs.answer: The most common method is installing the official PPA hosted on Launchpad for Ubuntu or Linux Mint, though it can also be built from source.

faqs.question: Who maintains Audio Recorder
faqs.answer: It is maintained as a community open-source project hosted on Launchpad rather than by a commercial company.

enrichment.short_description: AudioNodes is a browser-based and downloadable modular synthesizer and digital audio workstation that lets users patch together synths, effects, and audio files on a node-based canvas.
enrichment.seo_title: AudioNodes Review 2026: Modular Synth and DAW Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AudioNodes is a node-based modular synthesizer and audio editor for browser and desktop. See free features, AudioNodes HD pricing, and how it works in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AudioNodes is a modular synthesizer and digital audio workstation built around a node-based patching interface, developed by AudioNodes LLC. Rather than presenting a traditional linear timeline-only DAW, it lets users visually connect modules on a patching canvas, similar in spirit to a hardware modular synthesizer, to build custom synthesizers with oscillators, filters, envelopes, and modulation sources, route MIDI devices, edit and mix imported audio files, chain audio effects, and compose from scratch, including support for polyphonic patching, nested subpatches, and feedback chains. It is available both as an in-browser online editor at audionodes.com and as downloadable desktop software, and the software is currently in an actively developed alpha stage, with a public changelog documenting frequent updates. AudioNodes is free to use, including for commercial projects, with a generous free tier that places no limits on the number of nodes or tracks a project can contain, along with unlimited tracks on its Timeline UI, stereo audio rendering up to 48 kHz, and custom node creation; community sharing features also let users publish and use custom nodes built by others. A paid upgrade tier called AudioNodes HD is offered to help fund ongoing development, priced starting around 8.33 dollars per month when billed on an annual plan, with a free first month and the ability to cancel at any time; it unlocks advanced node settings, advanced timeline settings and markers, and other convenience and productivity features that are added on a rolling basis, while core creative functionality remains available in the free tier. If a user cancels their HD subscription, previously created projects and audio continue to work, though editing certain HD-exclusive node internals becomes unavailable again. Because it runs in the browser as well as on desktop, AudioNodes appeals to musicians, sound designers, and hobbyists who want a modular-synthesis-style workflow without buying dedicated modular hardware or licensing a heavyweight desktop-only DAW. It sits in a niche between browser-based audio tools and full modular software environments like VCV Rack, Bitwig's Grid, or Reaktor, differentiating itself through its combination of free access, browser availability, and a lightweight subscription that funds development rather than gating essential features.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AudioNodes
paragraphs: AudioNodes is a modular synthesizer and digital audio workstation built by AudioNodes LLC, centered on a node-based patching interface where users visually connect modules to build sound, similar to how a hardware modular synthesizer works.

It is available both as a browser-based online editor and as downloadable desktop software, and the project is in active, ongoing development, currently labeled as alpha with a regularly updated public changelog.
Rather than a purely linear timeline DAW, AudioNodes blends modular synthesis, audio file editing and mixing, MIDI routing, and multi-track composition into a single patch-based canvas.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can build custom synthesizers from oscillators, filters, envelopes, and modulation sources, including support for polyphonic patching, nested subpatches, and feedback chains for complex sound design.
The platform also supports importing, editing, and mixing audio files on a multi-track timeline, with cutting, slicing, and looping tools, alongside a library of ready-to-use audio effects.
Community sharing lets users create and distribute custom nodes, extending the built-in library with effects and instruments made by other users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AudioNodes is free to use, including for commercial projects, with a free tier that includes unlimited nodes and tracks, stereo rendering up to 48 kHz, and custom node creation.

AudioNodes HD is an optional paid subscription starting around 8.33 dollars per month when billed annually, with a free first month and the ability to cancel anytime. It unlocks advanced node and timeline settings and other productivity features, while previously created projects remain usable even after cancellation.

features.description: Create and share custom nodes with the broader AudioNodes community.

features.description: The free tier can be used for commercial projects without licensing fees.

Works in-browser with no installation required, plus a desktop option
Unique modular, node-based approach differentiates it from typical linear DAWs
Free tier is usable for commercial work
Affordable optional subscription for advanced features
cons: Software is currently in alpha, so bugs and breaking changes are possible
Node-based modular workflow has a learning curve for users used to linear DAWs
Browser version requires an internet connection to use
Some advanced settings and convenience features are gated behind the paid HD tier
No disclosed founding year or company size for AudioNodes LLC

faqs.question: Is AudioNodes free
faqs.answer: Yes, the core software is free to use, including for commercial projects, with an optional paid AudioNodes HD tier for advanced features.

faqs.question: How much does AudioNodes HD cost
faqs.answer: AudioNodes HD starts at around $8.33 per month when billed annually, with monthly billing also available and a free first month.

faqs.question: Can I use AudioNodes in a browser
faqs.answer: Yes, AudioNodes is available as an online editor that runs directly in the browser, in addition to downloadable desktop software.

faqs.question: What happens to my projects if I cancel AudioNodes HD
faqs.answer: Previously created projects and audio continue to work, but editing certain HD-exclusive node internals becomes unavailable again.

faqs.question: Is AudioNodes a full DAW or just a synthesizer
faqs.answer: It combines both: it functions as a modular synthesizer and as a digital audio workstation for editing, mixing, and composing with audio files.

faqs.question: Does AudioNodes support MIDI
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports MIDI device routing within patches.

faqs.question: Is AudioNodes suitable for beginners
faqs.answer: Its node-based, modular interface has a learning curve, but the free tier and browser access make it easy to experiment without commitment.

enrichment.short_description: Aura Frames makes WiFi-connected digital picture frames paired with a free companion app that lets family and friends share unlimited photos and videos, with no subscription required.
enrichment.seo_title: Aura Frames Review 2026: Prices, Models and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Aura Frames sells WiFi digital picture frames with free unlimited photo and video storage. Compare Carver, Aspen, Walden, and Ink prices and features for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Aura Frames, operated by Pushd Inc, is a New York-based company founded in 2012 by Abdur Chowdhury, previously Twitter's chief scientist and founder of Summize, along with CTO Eric Jensen. The company makes WiFi-connected digital picture frames paired with a free mobile app, and it has raised roughly 46.9 million dollars in funding across multiple rounds from investors including Spark Capital, SV Angel, Betaworks, and DCVC, with more recent rounds including a 26 million dollar raise in 2022 as the company approached 3 million app users and over a million frames sold. Aura's core product line includes the Carver, its most popular and most affordable model at 149 dollars for the base 10-inch version (with a matted variant around 179 dollars), the Mason, a smaller freestanding 9-inch dual-orientation frame, the Aspen, a 12-inch HD frame priced around 229 dollars, and the Walden, a larger 15-inch frame priced around 299 dollars. In late 2025 Aura introduced Ink, its first cordless, color e-paper frame using Spectra 6 e-ink technology, priced at 499 dollars, featuring a 13.3-inch display, an ultra-slim profile, and up to three months of battery life on a single charge. A defining part of Aura's value proposition is that there is no subscription fee for core use: every frame includes free, unlimited photo and video storage through Aura's cloud, with photos and videos added via the mobile app, email, a web uploader, an in-app scanner for physical prints, or direct sharing from a phone's camera roll, and the company states there are no recurring fees for using a frame's storage or receiving regular software feature updates. Several frame models, including Smith, Walden, Mason, Sawyer, and Carver, include built-in touch bar controls on one or two edges for swiping between photos, viewing photo details, or sending a heart reaction to whoever shared a photo, plus a motion sensor that allows gesture-based navigation by waving a hand near the frame. Aura's most direct competitor is Skylight, another well-known WiFi digital frame brand, along with Nixplay and PhotoShare; Aura is generally positioned as a premium, design-forward option with an emphasis on free unlimited storage as a differentiator against frames from brands that charge ongoing subscription fees for cloud storage. The product is targeted at consumers buying gifts for family members, particularly grandparents and relatives who live far away, as an easy way to receive and view photos without needing to manage a traditional photo-sharing app.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Aura Frames
paragraphs: Aura Frames, made by New York-based Pushd Inc, is a line of WiFi-connected digital picture frames paired with a free companion mobile app that lets family and friends share photos and videos directly to a frame from anywhere. The company was founded in 2012 by Abdur Chowdhury, formerly Twitter's chief scientist, and CTO Eric Jensen, and has raised roughly 46.9 million dollars in venture funding from investors including Spark Capital, SV Angel, Betaworks, and DCVC.
By late 2022 the company reported nearing 3 million app users and over a million frames sold, reflecting steady growth in the connected digital photo frame category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features and Product Lineup
paragraphs: Aura's frame lineup spans several sizes and price points: the Carver, its most popular and affordable model, starts at 149 dollars for the base 10-inch version; the Mason is a smaller 9-inch freestanding, dual-orientation frame; the Aspen is a 12-inch HD frame priced around 229 dollars; and the Walden is a larger 15-inch frame priced around 299 dollars. In late 2025, Aura launched Ink, its first cordless color e-paper frame using Spectra 6 e-ink technology, priced at 499 dollars, with up to three months of battery life on a single charge.

Every frame connects to the free Aura app, which supports free and unlimited photo and video storage, with content added via the app, email, a web uploader, an in-app document scanner for printed photos, or direct sharing from a phone's camera roll.

Several models, including Smith, Walden, Mason, Sawyer, and Carver, include touch bar controls for swiping between photos or reacting to a shared photo, plus a built-in motion sensor for gesture-based navigation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Aura frames are sold as one-time hardware purchases ranging from around 149 dollars for the entry-level Carver up to 499 dollars for the cordless Ink e-paper frame, with the Aspen and Walden models priced in between.

Unlike some competing digital frame brands, Aura does not charge a recurring subscription fee for core use. Unlimited cloud photo and video storage and regular software updates are included free with every frame.

faqs.answer: No. Aura frames include free, unlimited photo and video storage with no recurring subscription fee for core use.

faqs.answer: The Carver, Aura's most popular model, starts at 149 dollars for the base 10-inch version.

faqs.answer: Photos and videos can be added through the Aura mobile app, email, a web uploader, an in-app scanner for printed photos, or directly from a phone's camera roll.

faqs.answer: Aura Frames was founded in 2012 by Abdur Chowdhury, formerly Twitter's chief scientist, and CTO Eric Jensen.

faqs.answer: Both are WiFi digital picture frame brands; Aura differentiates itself with free unlimited storage included on every frame, while some competitors charge separate subscription fees for storage or extra features.

faqs.question: Do Aura frames support video, not just photos
faqs.answer: Yes, Aura frames support sharing and displaying short videos in addition to photos.

faqs.answer: Aura Frames is headquartered in New York, NY, USA.
